title: Derivatives from First Principles
subject: math
summary: Learn the derivative as the limit of average rates of change.

lessons.title: Derivative as a Limit
lessons.summary: Build the derivative from slopes between two points. Keep h nonzero until the limit step.
objectives: Observe average slope between two different inputs.
Use the difference quotient with h not equal to 0 before the limit is taken.
Explain the derivative as the value approached by those slopes.
Compute the derivative of f(x) = x^2 from first principles.
Avoid substituting h = 0 before the quotient is simplified.

blocks.title: Derivatives from First Principles
blocks.subtitle: Start with two different points. Then ask what slope they approach as the gap shrinks.

objectives.value: Observe a slope between two points first.

body.value: Pick two different inputs. The average rate of change is the slope between their output points:

keyIdeas.value: The two inputs must be different, so
A derivative asks what value these slopes approach when the inputs move together.

body.value: for the nearby input. The value
is the horizontal gap, and during algebra

takeaway.value: Calculate while
. Use the limit only after the quotient no longer divides by

blocks.caption: Formal rule. Compare f(x+h) to f(x), divide by the nonzero gap h, then let h approach 0.

blocks.title: Secant line approaching tangent slope
blocks.description: Notice the two marked points first. The secant line uses both points. If the second point moves toward x = 1, the secant slope approaches the tangent slope 2 at the fixed point.

body.value: Read the graph in this order: the curve, the fixed point, the nearby point, then the secant line. The quotient measures the slope of that secant line.

takeaway.value: The graph is a check on the formula
. It does not replace the algebra.

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = x^2
blocks.goal: Calculate f'(x) by simplifying before the limit is evaluated.

steps.label: Write the definition
steps.explanation: Start with the formal rule. The gap h is not zero yet.

steps.label: Substitute f(x) = x^2
steps.explanation: Replace f(x+h) with (x+h)^2. Replace f(x) with x^2.

steps.label: Expand the square
steps.explanation: Write out the numerator so the matching terms are visible.

steps.label: Combine like terms
steps.explanation: The terms x^2 and -x^2 add to zero.

steps.label: Factor h while h is not zero
steps.explanation: Each remaining numerator term has a factor h. This is the safe reason cancellation will work.

steps.label: Cancel the common factor
steps.explanation: Now the quotient no longer divides by h.

steps.label: Evaluate the limit
steps.explanation: Now h can approach 0. There is no division by h left.

blocks.interpretation: The result says: at input x, the tangent slope of y = x^2 is 2x.

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = 3x - 5
blocks.goal: Use the same definition for a linear function.

steps.label: Write the definition
steps.explanation: Use the same first-principles rule. Keep h nonzero during algebra.

steps.label: Substitute f(x) = 3x - 5
steps.explanation: Replace f(x+h) with 3(x+h)-5. Subtract the whole expression 3x-5.

steps.label: Expand and distribute the subtraction
steps.explanation: Expand 3(x+h). Then apply the minus sign to every term in 3x-5.

steps.label: Combine like terms
steps.explanation: The x terms cancel. The constants also cancel.

steps.label: Cancel h while h is not zero
steps.explanation: The numerator and denominator share the factor h. Cancel it before the limit step.

steps.label: Evaluate the limit
steps.explanation: The expression is already the constant 3.

blocks.interpretation: The result says: every point on y = 3x - 5 has tangent slope 3.

blocks.title: Forgetting to subtract the whole function
blocks.whyWrong: The entire value f(x) is being subtracted. The minus sign must apply to both 3x and -5.
blocks.correction: Keep f(x) in parentheses when substituting, then distribute the subtraction: -(3x-5) = -3x + 5.
blocks.checkPrompt: Did you subtract every term in f(x), including the constant term?

blocks.title: Substituting h = 0 too early
blocks.misconception: The limit h -> 0 means h can be replaced with 0 immediately.
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing ((x + 0)^2 - x^2) / 0 at the start of the calculation.
blocks.whyWrong: That creates division by zero before the expression has been simplified.
blocks.correction: Keep h nonzero while simplifying, cancel the common factor h, then evaluate the limit.
blocks.checkPrompt: Can the expression still contain division by h when you substitute h = 0?

body.value: Observe the quotient before the quiz. If the denominator is still

, cancel while
, then take the limit.

options.feedback: Correct. This removes division by h before h approaches 0.
Review the mistake block. Substituting h = 0 first creates division by zero.

text.value: Cancel the h in the denominator without factoring the numerator.

options.feedback: Review the factoring step. You can cancel h only after it is a common factor of the whole numerator and denominator.

questions.hint: Look for the step that removes division by h without pretending h already equals 0.

prompt.value: , what constant slope should the first-principles calculation give for

feedback.correct: Correct. The quotient simplifies to 3 before the limit is evaluated.
feedback.incorrect: Review the parentheses mistake. After distributing the subtraction, the quotient becomes 3h/h while h is nonzero, so the limit is 3.

questions.hint: After simplifying, the expression no longer depends on x or h.

items.value: A derivative starts with slopes between two different inputs.
. It means compare, divide by the nonzero gap, then take the limit.
, first principles give the constant slope

keyIdeas: The difference quotient compares two points over a nonzero gap h.
The limit describes the slope value approached as h moves toward 0.
For f(x) = x^2, first-principles algebra gives f'(x) = 2x.
For f(x) = 3x - 5, first-principles algebra gives the constant slope f'(x) = 3.

lessons.title: Derivative at a Point
lessons.summary: Keep one input fixed and calculate one tangent slope.
objectives: Compare a derivative function with one derivative value.
Use the point form of the first-principles definition with a fixed input.
Compute f'(2) for f(x) = x^2 from first principles.
Interpret the result as the tangent slope at (2, 4).
Avoid mixing the variable input x with the fixed input.

blocks.subtitle: Keep one point fixed. Let the nearby point move toward it.

blocks.title: Tangent slope at x = 2
blocks.description: Notice the fixed point (2, 4). The secant through (2, 4) and (3, 9) has slope 5. The tangent line at (2, 4) has slope 4, which is the value the secant slopes approach.

annotations.text: This nearby point gives the h = 1 secant.

body.value: Read the graph in this order: the fixed point, the nearby point, the secant line, then the tangent line. The fixed point stays at

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = x^2 at x = 2
blocks.goal: Calculate f'(2) without using a variable base input.

steps.label: Write the point definition
steps.explanation: Fix the base input at 2. Let the nearby input be 2 + h.

steps.label: Substitute f(x) = x^2
steps.explanation: Replace f(2+h) with (2+h)^2. Replace f(2) with 2^2.

steps.label: Expand the square
steps.explanation: Expand (2+h)^2 so the matching constants are visible.

steps.label: Combine like terms
steps.explanation: The constants 4 and -4 add to zero.

steps.label: Factor h while h is not zero
steps.explanation: Each remaining numerator term has a factor h. The gap is still nonzero.

steps.label: Cancel the common factor
steps.explanation: Now the expression no longer divides by h.

steps.label: Evaluate the limit
steps.explanation: Now h can approach 0 without division by zero.

blocks.interpretation: The result says: at the point (2, 4), the tangent slope of y = x^2 is 4.

blocks.title: Mixing the fixed input with the variable input
blocks.misconception: When computing f'(2), the numerator can still use the variable x.
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing ((x+h)^2-2^2)/h when the target is f'(2).
blocks.whyWrong: The base input has already been fixed at 2. Both function values must use 2 and 2 + h.
blocks.correction: Use ((2+h)^2-2^2)/h, simplify while h is nonzero, and then take the limit.
blocks.checkPrompt: Have you replaced every base input with 2 before simplifying?

means the base input is already

and
. Do not bring

options.feedback: Correct. The base input is fixed at 2, and the nearby input is 2 + h.
Review the mistake block. That mixes the variable input x with the fixed input 2.
Review the limit step. Substituting h = 0 before simplifying creates division by zero.

questions.hint: Keep one point at x = 2. Move the other point to x = 2 + h.

feedback.correct: Correct. The point difference quotient simplifies to 4 + h, so the limit is 4.
feedback.incorrect: Review the fixed-input substitution. The quotient must simplify to 4 + h before the limit is evaluated.

questions.hint: The tangent slope at x = 2 is the value approached by the secant slopes.

items.value: A derivative value fixes one input before the limit is taken.
, first principles give the one value

keyIdeas: A derivative value such as f'(2) fixes the base input before taking the limit.
The nearby input is 2 + h, where h is nonzero before the limit is evaluated.
For f(x) = x^2, first principles give f'(2) = 4.
The value f'(2) is the tangent slope at the point (2, 4).

lessons.title: Constant Function Derivative
lessons.summary: Use first principles to see why a function with no output change has derivative 0.
objectives: Observe a function whose output stays the same.
Use the difference quotient for f(x) = 7 while h is not equal to 0.
Explain why the numerator becomes 0.
Compute f'(x) = 0 for a constant function.
Avoid treating the constant output as a slope.

blocks.title: Constant Function Derivative
blocks.subtitle: When the output does not change, the average slope is zero before the limit step.

objectives.value: Observe two inputs with the same output.
Check the mistake: copying the output value as the slope.

blocks.title: Observe: same output at both inputs

body.value: measures output change, so it becomes

keyIdeas.value: The horizontal gap
is still nonzero during algebra.
The vertical change is
because both output values are

blocks.caption: Constant-function setup. The numerator is zero because both function values are 7.

blocks.title: Flat function has zero slope
blocks.description: Notice the two marked points first. Their outputs have the same height, so the secant line is horizontal and its slope is 0.

annotations.label: same output
annotations.text: The output change from x = 0 to x = 2 is 0.

body.value: Read the graph in this order: the flat line, the two marked points, then the horizontal secant line. The formula sees the same fact as

takeaway.value: The graph checks the numerator
. Keep
until the quotient is simplified.

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = 7
blocks.goal: Calculate f'(x) for a function whose output never changes.

steps.label: Write the definition
steps.explanation: Use the same first-principles rule. The input gap h is not zero yet.

steps.label: Substitute f(x) = 7
steps.explanation: Both f(x+h) and f(x) equal 7.

steps.label: Combine the output change
steps.explanation: The numerator is zero because the output did not change.

steps.label: Simplify while h is not zero
steps.explanation: Zero divided by any nonzero h is zero.

steps.label: Evaluate the limit
steps.explanation: The expression is already the constant 0.

blocks.interpretation: The result says: a flat function has tangent slope 0 at every input.

blocks.title: Treating the constant output as the slope
blocks.misconception: The derivative of f(x) = 7 should be 7 because the output is always 7.
blocks.whyWrong: The derivative measures output change per input change. The output value is 7, but the output change is 0.
blocks.correction: Use f(x+h)-f(x)=7-7=0, then the quotient is 0 while h is nonzero.
blocks.checkPrompt: Did you compare two outputs, or did you copy the output value?

body.value: Observe the numerator before the quiz. For

takeaway.value: If the two outputs are equal, the numerator is

options.feedback: Correct. The output does not change, so the numerator is 0.
Review the mistake block. The output value is 7, but the output change is 0.
The value h is the input gap. The numerator asks for output change.

questions.hint: Compare the two output values before dividing by h.

feedback.correct: Correct. The quotient simplifies to 0 before the limit is evaluated.
feedback.incorrect: Review the numerator. A constant function has output change 0, so the derivative is 0.

questions.hint: A flat graph has zero tangent slope.

items.value: A constant function has the same output at every input.

keyIdeas: A constant function has no output change between two inputs.
For f(x) = 7, the numerator f(x+h)-f(x) becomes 0.
Zero divided by nonzero h is zero before the limit is evaluated.
A flat function has derivative 0 at every input.
recallPrompts: What stays the same in a constant function?
Why does f(x+h)-f(x) become 0?
Where is h still required to be nonzero?
Why is the derivative 0 instead of 7?

lessons.title: Power Rule for Derivatives
lessons.summary: Use first principles to find a pattern for the derivative of x to a positive integer power.
objectives: Observe how the binomial expansion of (x + h)^n produces a leading n x^(n-1) h term.
Use first principles to compute the derivative of f(x) = x^3.
State the power rule for positive integer exponents.
Apply the power rule to compute derivatives of simple monomials.
Avoid bringing the exponent down without reducing it by 1.

blocks.title: Power Rule for Derivatives
blocks.subtitle: Watch the same factor appear each time you expand (x + h) raised to a small power.

objectives.value: Check the mistake: forgetting to reduce the exponent by

body.value: , first principles give a clean pattern: the derivative of

keyIdeas.value: The exponent comes down as a factor in front.
The exponent then drops by one.

body.value: by hand. The first term after

takeaway.value: The leading non-cancelling term of
is always

blocks.caption: Power rule. Bring the exponent down as a factor, then reduce the exponent by 1.

blocks.title: Cubic and its tangent at x = 1
blocks.description: Notice the cubic curve y = x^3 first. The marked point is (1, 1). The tangent line there has slope 3, which equals 3 times 1 squared.

annotations.text: The tangent slope here is 3, which matches 3 times 1 squared.

body.value: Read the graph in this order: the curve, the marked point, then the tangent line. The slope of the tangent line is

takeaway.value: The graph checks the rule. The algebra below explains why the rule holds for

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = x^3
blocks.goal: Use first principles to confirm the pattern for n = 3.

steps.label: Write the definition
steps.explanation: Use the first-principles rule. Keep h nonzero during algebra.

steps.label: Substitute f(x) = x^3
steps.explanation: Replace f(x+h) with (x+h)^3 and f(x) with x^3.

steps.label: Expand the cube
steps.explanation: Write out (x + h)^3 so the matching terms are visible.

steps.label: Combine like terms
steps.explanation: The terms x cubed and minus x cubed cancel.

steps.label: Factor h while h is not zero
steps.explanation: Every remaining numerator term has at least one factor of h.

steps.label: Cancel the common factor
steps.explanation: The quotient no longer divides by h.

steps.label: Evaluate the limit
steps.explanation: Now h can approach 0. The two h terms vanish.

blocks.interpretation: The result matches the pattern: for n = 3, the derivative is 3 x^(3-1) = 3 x^2.

blocks.title: Forgetting to reduce the exponent
blocks.misconception: The exponent only comes down as a factor; it does not change.
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing the derivative of x^3 as 3 x^3 instead of 3 x^2.
blocks.whyWrong: The first-principles calculation shows the leading non-cancelling term is 3 x^2 h, so after cancelling one h the surviving factor is x^2, not x^3.
blocks.correction: After bringing the exponent down, reduce the original exponent by 1: derivative of x^n is n x^(n - 1).
blocks.checkPrompt: Did the exponent drop by exactly one after the n came down?

body.value: Before answering, say the two moves out loud: bring

takeaway.value: Both moves must happen. Doing only one gives a wrong answer.

options.feedback: Correct. The 5 comes down as a factor and the exponent drops to 4.
Review the mistake block. The exponent must also drop by 1.
The exponent dropped, but the 5 must come down as a factor.

questions.hint: Bring the exponent down, then reduce the exponent by 1.

feedback.correct: Correct. First principles confirm the pattern for n = 3.
feedback.incorrect: Review the worked example. The first principles give 3 x squared.

questions.hint: Bring the 3 down, then reduce the exponent to 2.

keyIdeas: The exponent comes down as a factor and then drops by 1.
The binomial expansion gives a leading n x^(n-1) h term.
For f(x) = x^3, first principles give f'(x) = 3 x^2.
The pattern holds for every positive integer n.
recallPrompts: What are the two moves in the power rule?
Where does the factor n come from in the expansion?
Why does the exponent drop by exactly 1?
What does the graph at x = 1 show for f(x) = x^3?

lessons.title: Sum and Constant Multiple Rule
lessons.summary: See why the derivative of a sum is the sum of the derivatives, and why constants slide outside.
objectives: Observe that the difference quotient distributes across addition.
Use the sum rule to differentiate term by term.
Use the constant multiple rule to slide constants outside derivatives.
Combine the two rules with the power rule on a polynomial.
Avoid differentiating constants as if they were variables.

blocks.title: Sum and Constant Multiple Rule
blocks.subtitle: Differentiate term by term, and treat constants as multipliers.

objectives.value: Combine both rules with the power rule on a polynomial.

body.value: The difference quotient of a sum is the sum of the difference quotients. The limit of a sum is the sum of the limits when each exists. So derivatives add term by term.

keyIdeas.value: Differentiate each term separately, then add the results.
A constant multiplier stays attached to its term.

takeaway.value: Splitting the work is allowed. Each term keeps its constant factor.

blocks.caption: Sum rule and constant multiple rule, stated together.

blocks.title: Polynomial y = 4x^3 + 2x
blocks.description: Notice the polynomial first. The marked point is at x = 1, where the function value is 6 and the tangent slope is 14.

annotations.text: At x = 1 the tangent slope equals 12 + 2 = 14.

body.value: Each term contributes its own slope. At

takeaway.value: Term-by-term contributions add to the overall tangent slope

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = 4 x^3 + 2 x
blocks.goal: Combine the sum rule, constant multiple rule, and power rule on one polynomial.

steps.label: Split into separate terms
steps.explanation: The sum rule lets you differentiate each term on its own.

steps.label: Slide each constant outside
steps.explanation: The constant multiple rule moves the 4 and the 2 outside the derivative.

steps.label: Apply the power rule to each piece
steps.explanation: The derivative of x cubed is 3 x squared. The derivative of x is 1.

steps.label: Combine the results
steps.explanation: Multiply through and add.

blocks.interpretation: At x = 1 this gives 14, which agrees with the tangent slope on the graph.

blocks.title: Differentiating a constant as if it were a variable
blocks.misconception: The constant 4 in 4 x^3 has its own derivative that must be calculated.
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing the derivative of 4 x^3 as 4 + 3 x^2 instead of 4 * 3 x^2.
blocks.whyWrong: The 4 multiplies the term; it is not added to it. The constant multiple rule slides it outside the derivative, where it stays as a factor.
blocks.correction: Treat the 4 as a factor: derivative of 4 x^3 is 4 * derivative of x^3 = 4 * 3 x^2 = 12 x^2.
blocks.checkPrompt: Is the constant a factor in front of the term, or a separate added term?

body.value: Before answering, scan the polynomial term by term. Note each constant factor and each power.

takeaway.value: The two rules do most of the work; the power rule finishes each term.

feedback.correct: Correct. The two terms differentiate independently with their constant factors.
feedback.incorrect: Review the worked example. The result is 12 x squared plus 2.

questions.hint: Differentiate 4 x cubed and 2 x separately, then add.

items.value: The derivative of a sum is the sum of the derivatives.
A constant factor slides outside the derivative and stays attached.

keyIdeas: Derivatives distribute across addition.
Constant factors slide outside the derivative.
Combine these with the power rule to differentiate polynomials term by term.
For f(x) = 4 x^3 + 2 x, the derivative is f'(x) = 12 x^2 + 2.
recallPrompts: Why can the derivative of a sum be split term by term?
Where does a constant factor go when you differentiate?
What is the derivative of 2 x?
What is the derivative of 4 x^3?

lessons.title: Product Rule
lessons.summary: Use first principles to derive the rule for the derivative of a product of two functions.
objectives: Observe why (f g)' is not f' g'.
Use the add-and-subtract trick on f(x + h) g(x + h) - f(x) g(x).
State the product rule.
Apply the product rule to a simple polynomial product.
Avoid multiplying the two derivatives together.

sections.title: Observe Why Products Need a New Rule

blocks.title: Product Rule
blocks.subtitle: When two functions are multiplied, both contribute to the derivative.

objectives.value: Use the add-and-subtract trick in the numerator.
Apply the product rule to a polynomial.

body.value: When two functions are multiplied, the rate of change of the product comes from both factors. The product rule keeps both contributions explicit.

keyIdeas.value: One term differentiates the first factor and keeps the second factor as it is.
The other term keeps the first factor and differentiates the second factor.

body.value: to split the change into two pieces.

takeaway.value: Each piece isolates one factor's change while the other factor sits still.

blocks.caption: Product rule. Differentiate one factor at a time and add the results.

blocks.title: Product y = x^2 (x + 1)
blocks.description: Notice the cubic curve first. At x = 1 the function value is 2, and the tangent slope from the product rule is 5.

series.label: Function x squared times x plus 1

annotations.text: Product rule at x = 1: 2 times 2 plus 1 times 1 = 5.

body.value: Read the two parts of the product rule on the graph: the first term changes the cubic term's slope, and the second term changes the square term's slope. Both contribute.

takeaway.value: The two terms of the product rule are not the same; both must be written.

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = x^2 (x + 1)
blocks.goal: Apply the product rule and check by expanding.

steps.label: Name the two factors
steps.explanation: Let u(x) be x squared and v(x) be x plus 1.

steps.label: Find each factor's derivative
steps.explanation: The power rule gives the derivative of x squared as 2 x. The derivative of x plus a constant is 1.

steps.label: Apply the product rule
steps.explanation: Use u'(x) v(x) + u(x) v'(x).

steps.label: Simplify the result
steps.explanation: Distribute and combine like terms.

steps.label: Check by expanding first
steps.explanation: Expanding gives x^3 + x^2, whose derivative is also 3 x squared plus 2 x.

blocks.interpretation: At x = 1 the derivative is 5, matching the graph annotation.

blocks.title: Multiplying the two derivatives together
blocks.misconception: The derivative of a product is the product of the derivatives.
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing the derivative of x^2 (x + 1) as 2 x * 1 = 2 x.
blocks.whyWrong: That ignores the rate of change of each factor times the other factor. The first-principles derivation makes both terms unavoidable.
blocks.correction: Use the product rule: f' g + f g'. Differentiate one factor at a time and keep the other one as it is.
blocks.checkPrompt: Did you write two terms, one for each factor's derivative?

body.value: Before answering, write the product rule template

takeaway.value: Two terms must appear before any simplification.

prompt.value: Which expression equals the derivative of

options.feedback: Correct. This is the product rule applied step by step.
Review the mistake block. The derivative of a product is not the product of the derivatives.
This is only one of the two product-rule terms. The second term x^2 times 1 is missing.

questions.hint: Write f'g + fg' with f = x squared and g = x + 1.

items.value: A product of two functions has two contributions to its derivative.

keyIdeas: The derivative of a product is f' g + f g', not f' g'.
The add-and-subtract trick on the numerator splits the change into two pieces.
For f(x) = x^2 (x + 1), the product rule gives 3 x^2 + 2 x.
The result can be checked by expanding and differentiating term by term.
recallPrompts: Why does the product rule have two terms?
What is the add-and-subtract trick in the numerator?
Why is the derivative of a product not the product of the derivatives?
How does the polynomial check confirm the product rule?

lessons.title: Quotient Rule
lessons.summary: Use first principles to find the derivative of a quotient of two functions.
objectives: Observe that a quotient needs more than just dividing derivatives.
Apply the quotient rule to a simple ratio.
Use the quotient rule to derive the derivative of 1 over x.
Track the order f' g - f g' carefully.
Avoid swapping the numerator order or dropping the square in the denominator.

blocks.title: Quotient Rule
blocks.subtitle: Differentiate a ratio with one careful formula.

objectives.value: Apply the quotient rule to derive the derivative of

body.value: The quotient rule pairs the product-rule pattern with a denominator squared. The order of subtraction in the numerator is fixed: numerator-derivative first, denominator-derivative second.

keyIdeas.value: The numerator's derivative comes first.
The denominator is squared, but g itself is not differentiated twice.

body.value: Read the numerator left to right: derivative of top times bottom, then minus top times derivative of bottom. Swapping the order flips the sign.

takeaway.value: Mnemonic: low-d-high minus high-d-low, over the square of what is below.

blocks.caption: Quotient rule. The numerator follows a fixed order; the denominator is squared.

blocks.title: Reciprocal y = 1/x on the positive branch
blocks.description: Notice the curve y = 1/x for positive x. The tangent slope at x = 1 is -1, matching the prediction -1/x^2.

annotations.text: The tangent slope here is -1, which equals -1 over 1 squared.

body.value: Read the tangent line on the graph as a slope of negative one. The quotient rule predicts

takeaway.value: The formula and the graph agree only when the order in the numerator is correct.

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = 1/x
blocks.goal: Apply the quotient rule to a simple reciprocal.
blocks.given: f(x) = 1/x, with x not equal to 0

steps.label: Name the top and bottom
steps.explanation: Let u(x) be 1 and v(x) be x.

steps.label: Find each derivative
steps.explanation: The derivative of a constant is 0. The derivative of x is 1.

steps.label: Apply the quotient rule
steps.explanation: Substitute into the fixed numerator order, divided by v squared.

steps.label: Simplify
steps.explanation: The first term in the numerator is 0; the second is -1.

blocks.interpretation: At x = 1 this gives -1, which matches the tangent slope shown on the graph.

blocks.title: Swapping the order in the numerator
blocks.misconception: The quotient rule is symmetric, so the order in the numerator does not matter.
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing the numerator as f g' - f' g instead of f' g - f g'.
blocks.whyWrong: Swapping the order flips the sign of the result. The first-principles derivation fixes the order.
blocks.correction: Always write the numerator as derivative of top times bottom, minus top times derivative of bottom.
blocks.checkPrompt: Is f' g written before f g' in the numerator?

body.value: and fill in each blank in order.

takeaway.value: Filling the template in order avoids sign mistakes.

feedback.correct: Correct. The quotient rule gives negative one over x squared.
feedback.incorrect: Review the worked example. The result is negative one over x squared.

questions.hint: Apply the quotient rule with u = 1 and v = x.

items.value: The quotient rule fixes the order in the numerator and squares the denominator.
Order and squaring both matter; either mistake gives a wrong result.

keyIdeas: The quotient rule numerator is f' g - f g', in that order.
The denominator is g(x) squared.
For f(x) = 1/x, the derivative is -1/x^2.
Swapping the numerator order flips the sign.
recallPrompts: Which derivative comes first in the numerator?
Why is the denominator squared?
What is the derivative of 1/x?
Where is the domain restricted in the reciprocal example?

lessons.title: Chain Rule
lessons.summary: Differentiate compositions by multiplying the outer and inner derivatives.
objectives: Identify the outer function and the inner function in a composition.
Apply the chain rule to a polynomial composition.
Use a u-substitution view to keep the outer and inner derivatives separate.
Avoid forgetting the inner derivative factor.

blocks.title: Chain Rule
blocks.subtitle: When a function is wrapped inside another, both derivatives multiply.

objectives.value: Identify the outer and inner functions.
Multiply the outer derivative by the inner derivative.

blocks.title: Observe: a function inside a function

body.value: A composition first runs the inner function, then feeds the result into the outer function. Each layer changes at its own rate, and the chain rule multiplies the two rates.

keyIdeas.value: The outer derivative is taken with the inner expression kept untouched.
The inner derivative is then multiplied on the outside.

takeaway.value: Two derivatives, two multiplied factors, one composition.

blocks.caption: Chain rule. The outer derivative is evaluated at the inner function, then multiplied by the inner derivative.

blocks.title: Composition y = (x^2 + 1)^3
blocks.description: Notice the rapidly growing composition. At x = 1 the function value is 8 and the chain rule predicts tangent slope 24.

annotations.text: Chain rule at x = 1 gives 3 times 4 times 2 = 24.

body.value: Read both factors out loud: outer derivative is
. Their product gives the tangent slope.

takeaway.value: Both factors must be present for the chain rule to be complete.

blocks.title: Derivative of f(x) = (x^2 + 1)^3
blocks.goal: Use the chain rule with a clearly labeled outer and inner function.

steps.label: Label the outer and inner functions
steps.explanation: Outer is the cube function. Inner is x squared plus 1.

steps.label: Differentiate the outer function
steps.explanation: Use the power rule on u^3 with respect to u.

steps.label: Differentiate the inner function
steps.explanation: Use the power rule and the sum rule on x squared plus 1.

steps.label: Multiply the two derivatives
steps.explanation: Substitute u back and multiply by the inner derivative.

steps.label: Simplify
steps.explanation: Multiply the constants in front.

blocks.interpretation: At x = 1 this gives 6 * 1 * 4 = 24, matching the graph annotation.

blocks.title: Forgetting the inner derivative
blocks.misconception: If the outer function is already differentiated, the work is done.
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing the derivative of (x^2 + 1)^3 as 3 (x^2 + 1)^2 only.
blocks.whyWrong: The chain rule requires multiplying by the inner derivative; otherwise the rate of change of the inner expression is ignored.
blocks.correction: Always multiply by the derivative of the inside: 3 (x^2 + 1)^2 * 2 x = 6 x (x^2 + 1)^2.
blocks.checkPrompt: Did you multiply by the derivative of what is inside the parentheses?

body.value: Before answering, count the factors. A correct chain-rule answer for one composition has exactly two factors before simplification.

takeaway.value: Two factors before simplification: outer-derivative times inner-derivative.

prompt.value: Which expression equals the derivative of

options.feedback: Correct. This is the outer derivative times the inner derivative.
Review the mistake block. The inner derivative 2 x is missing.
This is only the inner derivative. The outer derivative is also needed.

questions.hint: Differentiate u cubed with respect to u, then multiply by the derivative of u with respect to x.

items.value: A composition has an outer function and an inner function.
The chain rule multiplies the outer derivative by the inner derivative.

keyIdeas: A composition runs the inner function first and feeds its output into the outer function.
The chain rule multiplies the outer derivative by the inner derivative.
For f(x) = (x^2 + 1)^3, the derivative is f'(x) = 6 x (x^2 + 1)^2.
Missing the inner derivative is the most common chain-rule mistake.
recallPrompts: What is the outer function in (x^2 + 1)^3?
What is the inner function in (x^2 + 1)^3?
Why does the inner derivative appear as a factor?
What value does the chain rule give at x = 1?

lessons.title: Tangent Line Equation
lessons.summary: Use a point and the derivative value at that point to write the line that touches the curve there.
objectives: Observe that a tangent line passes through (a, f(a)) with slope f'(a).
Write the tangent line equation in point-slope form.
Convert the tangent line to slope-intercept form.
Apply the tangent line equation to f(x) = x^2 at x = 1.
Avoid confusing the point value f(a) with the slope value f'(a).

sections.title: Observe One Point and One Slope

blocks.title: Tangent Line Equation
blocks.subtitle: One point on the curve and one slope from the derivative give a complete line.

objectives.value: Write the line in point-slope or slope-intercept form.

blocks.title: Observe: a line needs a point and a slope

body.value: uses two ingredients from the same input: the curve's value

keyIdeas.value: The point lives on the curve.
The slope comes from the derivative evaluated at the same input.

body.value: Before writing the equation, compute and write down both
as separate numbers. They are not the same.

takeaway.value: Confusing the two is the most common tangent-line mistake.

blocks.caption: Tangent line at x = a. The y-intercept is implicit; the form is point-slope.

blocks.title: Tangent to y = x^2 at x = 1
blocks.description: Notice the parabola first. The point (1, 1) is on the curve. The tangent line through that point has slope 2 and equation y = 2 x - 1.

annotations.text: The tangent line y = 2 x - 1 touches the parabola at this point.

body.value: Read the picture: the line touches the curve at exactly one nearby point and shares the same slope there. The equation captures that match using one point and one slope.

takeaway.value: The picture confirms the line that the formula produces.

blocks.title: Tangent to f(x) = x^2 at x = 1
blocks.goal: Use the tangent line equation to produce y = 2 x - 1.

steps.label: Compute the point
steps.explanation: Evaluate the function at a = 1.

steps.label: Compute the slope
steps.explanation: Use the power rule to get the derivative, then evaluate at a = 1.

steps.label: Substitute into the tangent line equation
steps.explanation: Use y = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a) with a = 1.

steps.label: Simplify to slope-intercept form
steps.explanation: Distribute and combine constants.

blocks.interpretation: The tangent line y = 2 x - 1 touches the parabola at (1, 1) with the same slope as the curve there.

blocks.title: Using f(a) as a slope
blocks.misconception: The number f(a) gives both the point and the slope.
blocks.incorrectStep: Writing the tangent line as y = f(a)(x - a) instead of y = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a).
blocks.whyWrong: The point and the slope come from different evaluations: f(a) is the y-value on the curve, and f'(a) is the rate of change at that input.
blocks.correction: Compute f(a) and f'(a) separately, then use both: y = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a).
blocks.checkPrompt: Did you write the point value and the slope value as two different numbers?

body.value: . Then plug both into the template.

takeaway.value: Two distinct numbers, one tangent line.

feedback.correct: Correct. The point (1, 1) and slope 2 give y = 2 x - 1.
feedback.incorrect: Review the worked example. The line is y = 2 x - 1.

questions.hint: Compute f(1) and f'(1) separately, then substitute.

prompt.value: Which form is the tangent line equation at

options.feedback: Correct. This uses both the point value and the slope value at the same input.
This is missing the constant f(a). The line would not pass through (a, f(a)).
Review the mistake block. This uses f(a) as if it were the slope.

questions.hint: The line uses both f(a) and f'(a).

items.value: A tangent line uses one point on the curve and the derivative slope at that point.

keyIdeas: The tangent line at x = a uses (a, f(a)) and slope f'(a).
The point-slope form is y = f(a) + f'(a)(x - a).
For f(x) = x^2 at x = 1, the tangent line is y = 2 x - 1.
Confusing f(a) with f'(a) breaks the tangent line.
recallPrompts: Which input is used for both the point and the slope?
How is the point-slope form built from f(a) and f'(a)?
Why does the tangent line need both f(a) and f'(a)?
What is the tangent line to y = x^2 at x = 1?

glossary.definition: The limiting value of average rates of change when it exists.
The expression that measures average rate of change from x to x + h.
A line through two points on a graph.
